events.text: The patient is a 45-year-old woman named Maria Elena Reyes. She is of Hispanic/Latina ethnicity, born in Mexico, residing in the southwestern United States. She works as a high school teacher. She is premenopausal, with no prior personal history of breast or ovarian cancer. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt diagnosed with breast cancer at age 52. She has no known BRCA mutation history at time of diagnosis. She is a never-smoker, drinks alcohol occasionally, and has a BMI of 26.4. She has two children.

At age 45 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition, cT2N1M0) INVASIVE DUCTAL CARCINOMA, NOS (MALIGNANT, PRIMARY) of the LEFT BREAST, upper outer quadrant (ICD-O site code C50.4). Histology code: 8500/3 (Infiltrating duct carcinoma, NOS). Grade: Grade 3 (Nottingham score 9/9; tubule formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic rate 3). Biomarkers: Estrogen receptor (ER): Negative (Allred score 0/8, 0% staining); Progesterone receptor (PR): Negative (Allred score 0/8, 0% staining); HER2 IHC: 1+ (negative); HER2 FISH: not amplified (HER2/CEP17 ratio 1.2); Ki-67: 85% (high proliferative index). Clinical presentation: palpable mass in left upper outer breast, approximately 3.2 cm by clinical exam. Axillary lymph node: one palpable, clinically suspicious left axillary node. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via core needle biopsy. Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) subtype confirmed. No evidence of distant metastasis on staging workup.

At age 45 years, the patient had a diagnostic mammogram and ultrasound of bilateral breasts and left axilla. Mammography showed a 3.4 cm irregular, spiculated mass in the left upper outer quadrant with associated architectural distortion. No suspicious microcalcifications. Ultrasound confirmed a 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm hypoechoic, irregular mass with posterior acoustic shadowing in the left breast at 10 o'clock position, 4 cm from the nipple. Left axillary ultrasound showed one morphologically abnormal lymph node at level I with cortical thickening to 8 mm and loss of fatty hilum, suspicious for nodal metastasis. Right breast and axilla: no suspicious findings. BI-RADS category 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy). Cancer status: cancer present (primary left breast mass and suspicious left axillary lymph node).

At age 45 years, the patient had a pathology result of type CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY (left breast mass, 10 o'clock position). Gross description: multiple tan-white core biopsy fragments, aggregate 1.4 cm. Microscopic description: invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3 (Nottingham grade 9/9). Tumor cells arranged in solid nests and sheets with minimal tubule formation. High nuclear grade with prominent nucleoli and frequent mitotic figures (>20 per 10 HPF). Stromal lymphocytic infiltrate (TIL score approximately 40%). No ductal carcinoma in situ identified in cores. Lymphovascular invasion: present. IHC panel: ER: negative (0%, Allred 0); PR: negative (0%, Allred 0); HER2: 1+ by IHC; HER2 FISH: not amplified. Ki-67: 85%. PD-L1 (22C3 antibody): Combined Positive Score (CPS) = 18 (positive; threshold >/=10 for pembrolizumab eligibility). Concurrent fine needle aspiration of suspicious left axillary lymph node: positive for malignant cells consistent with metastatic carcinoma.

At age 45 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast for staging purposes. CT chest: no pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Left axillary lymph node: 1.8 cm, morphologically abnormal, consistent with known metastasis. No right axillary adenopathy. CT abdomen: liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys unremarkable. No hepatic lesions suspicious for metastasis. No retroperitoneal adenopathy. CT pelvis: no suspicious pelvic lymphadenopathy or osseous lesions. Bone windows: no lytic or blastic lesions. Impression: Stage IIB disease, no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Cancer status: cancer present, locoregional disease only (primary left breast and left axillary node).

At age 45 years, the patient had an MRI of bilateral breasts with and without contrast (pre-treatment baseline). Left breast: 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm irregular, heterogeneously enhancing mass at 10 o'clock, 4 cm from the nipple. Rapid initial enhancement with washout kinetics. No skin thickening or chest wall involvement. No satellite nodules or additional suspicious enhancing foci. No nipple involvement. Left axilla: three level I lymph nodes with abnormal morphology, largest 1.9 cm (one more than identified by ultrasound). Right breast: no suspicious enhancement, no mass. Contralateral axilla: normal. MRI staging: cT2N1M0. Impression: locally advanced left breast primary with left axillary nodal involvement, no evidence of additional occult malignancy. Cancer status: cancer present.

At age 45 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for triple-negative breast cancer based on a specimen obtained from the left breast core needle biopsy (primary tumor). Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation Medicine FoundationOne CDx) revealed the following alterations: ACTIONABLE/CLINICALLY RELEVANT: TP53 p.R248W mutation (pathogenic, VAF 48%, co-mutation common in TNBC); PIK3CA wildtype; BRCA1 wildtype (tumor); BRCA2 wildtype (tumor); PTEN loss (homozygous deletion, copy number 0); AKT1 wildtype. ADDITIONAL ALTERATIONS: MYC amplification (copy number 18, high-level amplification); RB1 p.R579* (nonsense mutation, VAF 42%, loss of function); CDKN2A homozygous deletion; CDK6 amplification (copy number 7); CCND1 wildtype; MLL3 (KMT2C) p.E1285* (VAF 31%); ARID1A p.Q1378* (VAF 28%); SPEN p.Q1690fs (frameshift, VAF 22%); FAT1 p.R2428H (VAF 19%); NF1 p.E1803* (VAF 18%); NOTCH1 p.P2461fs (frameshift, VAF 15%). FUSIONS/REARRANGEMENTS: none detected. COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS: MYC gain/amplification (chr 8q24), CDK6 gain (chr 7q21), PTEN loss (chr 10q23), CDKN2A loss (chr 9p21), RB1 loss (chr 13q14). TMB: 8 mutations/Mb (intermediate). MSI: microsatellite stable (MSS). PD-L1 CPS 18 (as per IHC, consistent with immunotherapy eligibility). Germline testing (separate panel): BRCA1/2 wildtype; PALB2 wildtype; CHEK2 wildtype; ATM p.V2424G (variant of uncertain significance). HRD score (genomic instability): 42 (borderline, below threshold of 42.5 for olaparib eligibility per gBRCA-negative criteria). Note: Given TP53 mutation, MYC amplification, RB1 loss, and CDKN2A deletion, this tumor has a genomic profile consistent with high-grade, highly aggressive TNBC.

At age 45 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment and new patient oncology consultation. Case reviewed by breast medical oncology, surgical oncology, radiation oncology, and pathology. Diagnosis: Stage IIB (cT2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer, grade 3, left breast. PD-L1 CPS 18 (positive). KI-67 85%. Patient is premenopausal, ECOG performance status 0. No comorbidities. Plan: neoadjuvant systemic therapy with intent to downstage and assess pathologic response, then surgery. Recommended regimen: pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks plus dose-dense AC (doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 q2 weeks x 4 cycles) followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 x 12 doses with pembrolizumab q3 weeks, based on KEYNOTE-522 trial data. Pegfilgrastim support planned. Patient counseled on goals of therapy, expected toxicities (nausea, fatigue, alopecia, neuropathy, immune-related adverse events), fertility preservation (patient declined oocyte cryopreservation after counseling), and surgical options post-neoadjuvant therapy. Patient consented and is eager to proceed. Restaging MRI planned after completion of neoadjuvant therapy. Cancer present, initiating treatment.

At age 45 years, the patient received neoadjuvant-intent therapy: Cycle 1 of pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on day 1 (q3 week schedule) combined with dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 IV (AC chemotherapy, q2 week schedule). Pegfilgrastim 6 mg subcutaneous injection administered day 2. Pre-medications: ondansetron, dexamethasone, lorazepam. This initiated the AC phase (4 planned cycles, q2 weeks) with pembrolizumab running concurrently on a q3-week cycle. Day 1 of neoadjuvant treatment.

At age 45 years (approximately 2 weeks into treatment), the patient reported Grade 2 nausea and Grade 1 fatigue following Cycle 1 of AC. She also developed Grade 1 alopecia, which progressed to Grade 2 (complete scalp hair loss) by week 3. Anti-emetic regimen optimized: added prochlorperazine as breakthrough agent and olanzapine 5 mg nightly x 3 days per cycle. No febrile neutropenia. CBC nadir: ANC 1.2 x 10^9/L (Grade 2 neutropenia), recovered appropriately by day 14. No dose modifications required. No immune-related adverse events noted at this time.

At age 45 years, the patient received Cycles 2, 3, and 4 of dose-dense AC (doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide) q2 weeks with pegfilgrastim support, and pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on the schedule aligned with cycles 1 and 3 of AC (i.e., pembrolizumab doses 1, 2 administered during the AC phase). All four AC cycles administered at full dose. Cycle 4 AC completed approximately 8 weeks from initiation. Tolerance was acceptable with optimized antiemetics. No febrile neutropenia, no Grade 3/4 toxicity during AC phase. Patient transitioned to paclitaxel phase.

At age 45 years (during the paclitaxel phase, approximately week 14 of neoadjuvant therapy), the patient developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, bilateral hands and feet, described as tingling and mild numbness. Weekly paclitaxel was dose-reduced by 20% (from 80 mg/m2 to 64 mg/m2) for weeks 9-12 of paclitaxel per institutional protocol. Additionally, the patient developed Grade 2 immune-related arthralgia/arthritis affecting bilateral knee and wrist joints, attributed to pembrolizumab. Rheumatology consultation obtained. Prednisone 20 mg daily initiated with plan to taper over 4 weeks. Pembrolizumab was held for one cycle. After clinical improvement to Grade 1 with steroids, pembrolizumab was resumed at full dose. No colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, or endocrinopathy identified.

At age 45 years, the patient completed the paclitaxel phase: weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 (with dose reduction to 64 mg/m2 for weeks 9-12 as noted) x 12 total doses administered over approximately 12 weeks, with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks (4 additional doses during this phase, for a total of 6 pembrolizumab doses during neoadjuvant therapy). Full course of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/AC/paclitaxel completed. Total neoadjuvant therapy duration: approximately 20 weeks. Patient referred back to surgical oncology for definitive surgery.

At age 45 years (post-neoadjuvant, pre-surgery), the patient had an MRI of bilateral breasts with and without contrast for restaging. Left breast: residual irregular enhancing mass measuring 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm at 10 o'clock (previously 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm), representing a partial radiographic response (approximately 60% size reduction). Residual heterogeneous enhancement pattern, no washout kinetics identified in residual area (pattern changed from pre-treatment). Left axilla: previously abnormal level I lymph nodes now appear markedly improved; the largest node measures 0.7 cm with partial fatty hilum restoration, though one node remains borderline. No new suspicious lesions in either breast. No chest wall or skin involvement. Impression: partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant therapy, residual left breast mass with uncertain but possible residual nodal disease. Cancer status: cancer likely still present (residual), responding to therapy (partial response by imaging).

At age 45 years, the patient underwent left breast surgery and axillary staging. Procedure: left partial mastectomy (lumpectomy/breast-conserving surgery) with sentinel lymph node biopsy and left axillary lymph node dissection (ALND). The surgical oncologist performed wire-localized excision of the residual left breast mass with tumor bed clip localization. Sentinel lymph node biopsy identified 3 sentinel nodes; intraoperative frozen section of one sentinel node showed metastatic carcinoma, prompting completion ALND. Surgical findings: firm, ill-defined 1.5 cm residual tumor bed in left upper outer quadrant. Total lymph nodes removed: 16 (3 sentinel, 13 additional axillary). Estimated blood loss: 80 mL. No complications. Specimen sent to pathology. Closure performed in standard fashion. Post-operative recovery uneventful.

At age 45 years, the patient had a pathology result of type SURGICAL RESECTION (left partial mastectomy and axillary lymph node dissection). Gross description: left breast partial mastectomy specimen, 7.5 x 6.0 x 4.2 cm. Tumor bed identified with clip. Residual tumor: 1.2 cm focus of residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, at the excision site. Margins: all surgical margins negative (closest margin 4 mm posterior). Residual DCIS: not identified. Lymphovascular invasion: present in residual tumor. Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes in residual tumor: 15% (reduced from 40% pre-treatment). Treatment effect: moderate (Miller-Payne grade 3, approximately 50-75% reduction in tumor cellularity; RCB class II). Axillary lymph nodes: 3 of 16 lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (3/16); largest metastatic deposit 0.8 cm; no extranodal extension. ypT1cN1M0, Stage IIA (post-neoadjuvant). Pathologic assessment: NON-pCR (residual invasive disease present). Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) score: 2.8 (RCB-II, intermediate residual disease). IHC on residual tumor: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 IHC 1+ (negative), consistent with persistent TNBC phenotype.

At age 45 years (post-surgical, approximately 6 weeks after surgery), the patient had a post-operative oncology visit. Wound healing satisfactory. Pathology reviewed: ypT1cN1M0, RCB-II, non-pCR. Discussion of adjuvant therapy options. Given residual disease after neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/chemotherapy, plan for: (1) adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 twice daily x 14 days every 21 days x 8 cycles (per CREATE-X trial), and (2) continuation of pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks to complete 1 year total (approximately 9 additional doses to reach 17 total pembrolizumab doses, consistent with KEYNOTE-522 protocol). Radiation oncology referral placed for adjuvant radiation (post-mastectomy/post-lumpectomy radiation to left breast and nodal basins given 3+ positive nodes). Patient tolerated surgical recovery well. ECOG PS 1. She has begun to experience lingering Grade 1 neuropathy from paclitaxel. Alopecia improving (early regrowth noted). Cancer status: post-surgery, residual disease confirmed, starting adjuvant treatment.

At age 45 years (approximately 8 weeks post-surgery), the patient underwent adjuvant radiation therapy to the left breast and regional lymphatics. Radiation technique: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to left breast and left chest wall (tumor bed boost included). Fields: whole breast, supraclavicular fossa, axillary levels I-III, internal mammary chain. Dose: whole breast 50 Gy in 25 fractions (2 Gy/fraction, 5 days/week over 5 weeks), followed by tumor bed boost 10 Gy in 5 fractions. Regional nodal irradiation: supraclavicular 50 Gy in 25 fractions, axillary 46 Gy in 23 fractions. Total RT duration: approximately 6 weeks. Radiation was sequenced to begin concurrently with capecitabine initiation (capecitabine held during radiation per institutional protocol to minimize skin toxicity, then resumed). Pembrolizumab continued on schedule during radiation.

At age 45 years (during radiation therapy, approximately week 4 of RT), the patient developed Grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left breast and axilla with moist desquamation in the axillary fold. Managed with silver sulfadiazine cream, moisture-wicking dressings, and treatment break of 3 days. Completed radiation without further interruption. She also developed Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis (mild cough, no dyspnea, no infiltrate on CXR) approximately 6 weeks after completion of radiation, managed conservatively with low-dose prednisone 10 mg daily x 3 weeks with taper. No significant fatigue beyond baseline. Pembrolizumab was continued throughout radiation as pneumonitis was mild and attributed to radiation rather than immune-related etiology.

At age 45 years, the patient initiated adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily (days 1-14, every 21 days), beginning approximately 10 weeks post-surgery (after completion of radiation). Target: 8 cycles total. Concurrent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks continued (adjuvant maintenance phase, to complete approximately 1 year total from initiation of pembrolizumab). Uridine diphosphate glucuronosyltransferase (DPYD) genotyping performed prior to capecitabine: no pathogenic variant identified (DPYD wildtype). Patient counseled on hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, mucositis, and capecitabine-drug interactions.

At age 45 years (Cycle 3 of adjuvant capecitabine, approximately 3 months into adjuvant therapy), the patient developed Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): bilateral palmar and plantar erythema, swelling, and pain interfering with daily activities. Capecitabine dose reduced to 750 mg/m2 twice daily (25% dose reduction). Initiated urea-based emollient cream (Eucerin) to hands and feet twice daily, vitamin B6 supplementation, and avoidance of friction/heat. Grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools per day above baseline) also noted in Cycle 3, managed with loperamide and oral hydration; resolved within 5 days. No Grade 3/4 toxicities. No further pembrolizumab immune-related adverse events during this phase.

At age 45 years (approximately 6 months post-surgery, during adjuvant therapy), the patient had a surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. CT chest: no new pulmonary nodules. Post-radiation changes in left lung base (minor ground-glass opacity, consistent with prior radiation pneumonitis, resolving). No pleural effusion. Left axillary surgical bed: post-surgical changes, no new adenopathy. CT abdomen and pelvis: no hepatic lesions, no peritoneal nodules, no retroperitoneal or pelvic adenopathy. No ascites. Skeletal survey (bone windows): no new lytic or blastic lesions. Impression: no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Post-treatment changes only. Cancer status: no cancer detected on imaging, consistent with no evidence of disease (NED).

At age 45 years (approximately 7 months post-surgery), the patient had a routine oncology visit during adjuvant therapy. She is currently on Cycle 5 of capecitabine (dose-reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID) and has received 14 of 17 planned pembrolizumab doses total. Tolerating therapy reasonably well. Hand-foot syndrome improved to Grade 1 on dose-reduced capecitabine. Peripheral neuropathy Grade 1, stable. No new immune-related adverse events. Thyroid function tests: TSH 7.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated, new finding); free T4 low normal. Consistent with immune-mediated hypothyroidism (pembrolizumab-related). Endocrinology consultation placed. Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily initiated. Repeat TSH planned in 6 weeks. ECOG PS 1. Surveillance imaging (CT) reviewed - no evidence of disease. Cancer status: no evidence of disease, on adjuvant treatment.

At age 45 years (approximately 8 months post-surgery), the patient was confirmed to have Grade 1-2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism secondary to pembrolizumab. TSH stabilized at 4.2 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (dose uptitrated from 50 mcg). Per KEYNOTE-522 protocol guidance, hypothyroidism managed with hormone replacement; pembrolizumab continued without interruption as endocrinopathy is an expected manageable immune-related adverse event not requiring drug discontinuation. Patient asymptomatic from hypothyroidism. No other immune-related adverse events identified. Pembrolizumab maintenance continued.

At age 46 years, the patient completed all 8 cycles of adjuvant capecitabine (Cycle 8 completed) and received the final (17th) dose of pembrolizumab to complete approximately 1 year of total pembrolizumab therapy (counting neoadjuvant + adjuvant doses). Capecitabine was administered at reduced dose (750 mg/m2 BID) for cycles 3-8 due to hand-foot syndrome. All planned adjuvant therapy completed. Patient transitioned to active surveillance. Levothyroxine for immune-mediated hypothyroidism continued indefinitely.

At age 46 years (approximately 12 months post-surgery, end of adjuvant therapy), the patient had a surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast and bilateral breast MRI. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Post-treatment changes in left lung stable. No new lymphadenopathy, no hepatic lesions, no osseous lesions. Breast MRI: left breast surgical site with expected post-operative and post-radiation changes; no suspicious new enhancement. Right breast: no suspicious enhancement. Bilateral axillae: no pathologic adenopathy. Impression: no evidence of local recurrence or distant metastasis. Cancer status: no cancer detected, no evidence of disease (NED).

At age 46 years (12-month post-surgery surveillance visit), the patient had a routine oncology follow-up. She has completed all planned neoadjuvant and adjuvant therapies. She is now on active surveillance. Performance status: ECOG 0 (returned to full activity, teaching full-time). Peripheral neuropathy: resolved to Grade 0 (complete resolution). Hand-foot syndrome: resolved. Alopecia: complete hair regrowth. Hypothyroidism: stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH within normal limits. She reports improved energy and quality of life. Psychosocial: patient engaged with peer support group for young women with breast cancer; seeing a therapist for cancer-related anxiety and body image concerns; no active depression. Reproductive discussion: patient interested in future pregnancy considerations; referred to reproductive endocrinology for counseling on ovarian function and pregnancy safety post-TNBC. Menstrual cycles resumed post-chemotherapy (chemotherapy-associated amenorrhea resolved at 9 months). Plan: clinical exam and labs every 3-4 months x 2 years, then every 6 months. Annual bilateral breast MRI and mammography. Continue levothyroxine. Genetic counseling referral for updated BRCA testing with expanded panel given new family history information (maternal cousin recently diagnosed with ovarian cancer). Cancer status: no evidence of disease.

At age 46 years (18-month post-surgery surveillance), the patient had a surveillance bilateral breast MRI. Left breast: stable post-operative/post-radiation changes at surgical site. No new suspicious enhancement. No mass or architectural distortion. Right breast: no suspicious enhancement. Bilateral axillae: no pathologic adenopathy. Compared to prior MRI (12 months post-surgery): no interval change. Impression: no evidence of local recurrence. Cancer status: no cancer detected.

At age 46 years (18-month post-surgery surveillance), the patient had a routine oncology visit. She remains without evidence of disease. ECOG PS 0. No new symptoms. Continues levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal). Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 0 (fully resolved). Expanded germline panel resulted: BRCA1 wildtype, BRCA2 wildtype, PALB2 wildtype, RAD51C wildtype, RAD51D wildtype, BRIP1 wildtype, ATM p.V2424G (variant of uncertain significance, classification unchanged). Genetic counselor discussed implications with patient; no change in screening or management recommended based on VUS. Patient continues annual mammography and MRI surveillance per NCCN high-risk guidelines. She reports good quality of life. She has returned to all pre-diagnosis activities. Discussion of ongoing cardiovascular surveillance given prior anthracycline exposure: baseline echocardiogram performed (LVEF 62%, normal). Plan: continue surveillance per schedule. Cancer status: no evidence of disease at 18 months post-surgery.

documents.text:
BILATERAL BREAST MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — POST-NEOADJUVANT THERAPY RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
45-year-old premenopausal woman with known left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative subtype (ER/PR/HER2-negative), originally staged as cT2N1M0 (Stage IIB). She has completed full-course neoadjuvant systemic therapy consisting of dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide (AC) x 4 cycles followed by weekly paclitaxel x 12 doses, all administered in combination with pembrolizumab per KEYNOTE-522 protocol. Total neoadjuvant treatment duration approximately 20 weeks. This examination is requested for pre-surgical restaging and to assess treatment response prior to planned left breast surgery.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the bilateral breasts was performed on a dedicated 3.0 Tesla breast MRI unit with a bilateral breast surface coil. Sequences acquired include axial T2-weighted fat-suppressed, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, and dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) sequences before and at multiple time points following the intravenous administration of 0.1 mmol/kg gadobutrol (Gadavist). Subtraction images, MIP reconstructions, and time-intensity kinetic analysis were performed. Images are compared to prior breast MRI obtained at pre-treatment baseline.

FINDINGS:

LEFT BREAST:
There has been a marked reduction in the size and conspicuity of the known primary malignancy since the pre-treatment baseline examination. At the 10 o'clock position, approximately 4 cm from the nipple, there is a residual irregular area of enhancement measuring approximately 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm in the axial, craniocaudal, and AP dimensions, respectively (previously 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm at baseline). The residual enhancing focus demonstrates a heterogeneous, somewhat spiculated morphology with irregular margins, though with considerably reduced volume and signal intensity compared to the pre-treatment study. Importantly, the previously brisk rapid-initial-enhancement with washout kinetics has largely resolved; the residual area now demonstrates a slower, more persistent enhancement pattern, which may reflect a combination of treatment-related fibrosis and residual viable tumor — this kinetic change alone is not sufficient to exclude residual malignancy. There is no clearly defined enhancing mass with well-organized washout remaining, but the morphology of the residual focus does not allow confident exclusion of residual invasive carcinoma.

A titanium clip marking the original tumor bed is identified and corresponds to the area of residual enhancement, confirming appropriate localization.

No secondary satellite nodules or additional suspicious enhancing foci are identified in the left breast. There is no extension to the overlying skin or nipple-areolar complex. No chest wall involvement. No skin thickening or edema. No post-treatment abscess or fluid collection. Mild diffuse background parenchymal enhancement is present, expected in the context of this patient's premenopausal status.

Diffusion-weighted imaging: The residual enhancing area demonstrates moderately restricted diffusion with an ADC value of approximately 0.82 x 10^-3 mm2/s, which remains below threshold values typically associated with benign tissue, supporting the possibility of residual viable tumor rather than purely fibrotic change.

LEFT AXILLA:
Previously identified morphologically abnormal level I axillary lymph nodes have undergone substantial improvement in appearance. Three level I lymph nodes were identified on the baseline study, with the largest measuring 1.9 cm and exhibiting frankly abnormal cortical morphology without identifiable fatty hilum. On this examination, the largest level I node now measures approximately 0.7 cm and demonstrates partial restoration of the central fatty hilum, a substantial change from baseline. A second level I node measuring approximately 0.8 cm shows thin cortex and a suggestion of a fatty hilum, appearing borderline normal. The third previously identified node is no longer discretely identifiable as a morphologically abnormal structure. No new pathologically enlarged axillary nodes at level I, II, or III.

While the degree of nodal improvement is notable and represents a meaningful treatment response, at least one borderline level I node remains, and the clinical significance of this finding in the post-treatment setting is uncertain. Pathologic nodal staging at surgery is recommended for definitive assessment.

RIGHT BREAST:
No suspicious mass, architectural distortion, or abnormal enhancement identified throughout the right breast parenchyma. Background parenchymal enhancement is symmetric and mild to moderate. No right axillary adenopathy. No suspicious right internal mammary or supraclavicular nodes.

BILATERAL INTERNAL MAMMARY REGIONS:
No internal mammary adenopathy identified bilaterally.

SKIN AND NIPPLE-AREOLAR COMPLEX:
Skin thickness is within normal limits bilaterally. No skin retraction, nipple inversion, or enhancement of the nipple-areolar complex on either side.

POST-PROCESSING AND KINETIC ANALYSIS:
Subtraction images confirm the residual enhancing area in the left breast is real and not artifactual. Kinetic curve analysis across the residual focus is heterogeneous: predominantly persistent pattern with a small component of plateau kinetics. No clear dominant washout curve persists, in contrast to the pre-treatment study. This evolution in kinetic behavior is consistent with partial treatment response but cannot reliably distinguish fibrosis from residual viable malignancy.

COMPARISON:
Compared to the pre-treatment bilateral breast MRI, the primary lesion has decreased substantially in all dimensions (estimated volumetric reduction of approximately 87-90%), and the left axillary nodal burden has markedly decreased. However, a residual enhancing focus with morphologic and diffusion characteristics suspicious for residual viable tumor persists at the clip-marked site.

IMPRESSION:

1. Substantial radiographic response to neoadjuvant systemic therapy (pembrolizumab + AC + paclitaxel) within the left breast primary site. The residual irregular enhancing focus at 10 o'clock, now measuring approximately 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm (previously 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm at baseline), is compatible with residual viable tumor though treatment-induced fibrosis cannot be fully excluded on imaging alone. Clip in place; tumor bed is well-defined for surgical targeting.

2. Markedly improved appearance of the left axillary lymph nodes compared to baseline, with partial restoration of fatty hila in the previously abnormal nodes. At least one borderline level I node persists; definitive nodal staging will require surgical-pathologic correlation.

3. No evidence of new suspicious lesions in the left breast, right breast, or either axilla.

4. No imaging evidence of chest wall, skin, or nipple-areolar complex involvement.

5. Findings are consistent with a partial radiographic treatment response; complete pathologic response cannot be confirmed or excluded by imaging, and surgical resection with pathologic assessment of the residual specimen remains the standard for definitive staging.

Communicated to referring oncology team at time of report finalization.

DEPARTMENT OF PATHOLOGY AND LABORATORY MEDICINE
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

Patient Name: Maria Elena Reyes
Medical Record Number: [MRN on file]
Accession Number: SP-[ACCESSION]
Ordering Physician: Surgical Oncology / Breast Surgery Service
Clinical Service: Breast Surgery
Procedure: Left Partial Mastectomy (Lumpectomy) with Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy and Left Axillary Lymph Node Dissection
Clinical Indication: Residual left breast carcinoma following neoadjuvant chemotherapy plus immunotherapy (pembro + dose-dense AC x4 cycles followed by weekly PTX x12; KEYNOTE-522 schema). Pre-surgical imaging: residual 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm enhancing mass at 10 o'clock left breast by MRI. Sentinel lymph node biopsy with intraoperative frozen section positive for metastatic carcinoma; completion axillary lymph node dissection performed.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINAL DIAGNOSIS
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

A. LEFT BREAST, PARTIAL MASTECTOMY (LUMPECTOMY):
   - Residual INVASIVE DUCTAL CARCINOMA, NOS (INFILTRATING DUCT CARCINOMA), GRADE 3
     (Nottingham Score: 7/9; tubule formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic rate 1)
   - Residual tumor size: 1.2 cm (greatest dimension, microscopic measurement)
   - Treatment effect present: MODERATE (Miller-Payne Grade 3; estimated 50-75% reduction
     in overall tumor cellularity compared to pre-treatment biopsy)
   - Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) calculated: see comment
   - Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS): NOT IDENTIFIED
   - Lymphovascular invasion: PRESENT (in residual viable tumor foci)
   - Perineural invasion: NOT IDENTIFIED
   - Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs, residual tumor): approximately 15% stromal TILs
     (decreased from 40% at pre-treatment core needle biopsy)
   - Skin: NOT INVOLVED
   - Skeletal muscle/chest wall: NOT INVOLVED
   - Surgical margins: ALL MARGINS NEGATIVE FOR INVASIVE CARCINOMA AND DCIS
       Posterior (deep) margin: 4 mm (closest margin)
       Superior margin: 8 mm
       Inferior margin: >10 mm
       Medial margin: >10 mm
       Lateral margin: 12 mm
       Anterior (superficial) margin: >10 mm

B. LEFT AXILLA, SENTINEL LYMPH NODES (3 nodes submitted, as identified by blue dye
   and radiotracer):
   - Sentinel Lymph Node #1: POSITIVE for metastatic carcinoma (macrometastasis, 0.8 cm
     deposit; no extranodal extension)
   - Sentinel Lymph Node #2: NEGATIVE for carcinoma (reactive changes only)
   - Sentinel Lymph Node #3: NEGATIVE for carcinoma; isolated tumor cells present
     (cytokeratin IHC positive, single cell cluster < 0.2 mm; classified as pN0(i+))

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Specimen A -- LEFT BREAST:
Serial sections through the tumor bed reveal extensive treatment-related changes
consisting of stromal fibrosis, hemosiderin deposition, foamy macrophages (lipid-laden
histiocytes), elastosis, and scattered foci of chronic inflammation. Within this
fibrotic background, there are residual viable clusters and small nests of malignant
epithelial cells, consistent with invasive ductal carcinoma. The residual tumor cells
demonstrate high nuclear grade with prominent nucleoli and moderate mitotic activity
(markedly reduced compared to pre-treatment biopsy). Residual tumor is present across
multiple sections spanning a tumor bed area of approximately 1.5 x 1.2 cm; the
largest continuous focus of viable invasive carcinoma measures 1.2 cm. Tubule formation
is absent (score 3); nuclear pleomorphism is high (score 3); mitotic rate is low in
residual viable areas (1-2 per 10 HPF, score 1); Nottingham score 7/9, Grade 3.
Stromal tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (sTILs) are present at approximately 15%
(reduced from pre-treatment 40%), consistent with partial immune response. No
residual DCIS is identified. Lymphovascular invasion is present, identified as
residual tumor emboli within small vascular spaces at the periphery of the tumor bed.
All margins are free of invasive carcinoma; the posterior margin is the closest at 4 mm.

Specimen B -- SENTINEL LYMPH NODES:
Node #1: Sections demonstrate a 0.8 cm deposit of metastatic ductal carcinoma within
the nodal parenchyma, with surrounding stromal desmoplasia. Significant fibrous
replacement of approximately 50% of the nodal architecture, consistent with prior
treatment response. No extranodal extension into perinodal adipose tissue identified.
The metastatic cells are morphologically identical to the primary tumor and are
confirmed by cytokeratin AE1/AE3 immunostaining. Node #2: Reactive follicular
hyperplasia and sinus histiocytosis; no malignant cells identified on H&E or
cytokeratin IHC. Node #3: H&E sections show reactive changes only. Cytokeratin
(AE1/AE3) immunostaining reveals a single cluster of 3-4 isolated tumor cells
(<0.2 mm aggregate dimension), classified as isolated tumor cells (ITC),
pN0(i+). No evidence of viable macrometastasis in this node.

Specimen C -- AXILLARY LYMPH NODE DISSECTION:
Node #4 (Level I): 0.6 cm metastatic ductal carcinoma; treatment-related fibrosis
present within and around the deposit; no extranodal extension. Node #9 (Level II):
0.3 cm micrometastasis; cytokeratin IHC confirms epithelial cells; no extranodal
extension; fibrosis present. Eleven remaining nodes: no malignant cells identified;
varying degrees of reactive sinus histiocytosis, follicular hyperplasia, and
treatment-related fibrous change.

COMMENT
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

This case is reviewed in the context of prior neoadjuvant therapy (pembro
200 mg IV q3 weeks plus dose-dense Adriamycin/Cytoxan x4 cycles followed
by weekly Taxol x12 doses). The pathologic findings are consistent with a
partial pathologic response (non-pCR). The residual invasive carcinoma maintains the
pre-treatment immunophenotype (ER-/PR-/HER2 1+), confirming persistent triple-negative
breast cancer biology without phenotypic conversion.

The Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) index of 2.8 (RCB-II, intermediate) was calculated
using the MD Anderson RCB calculator methodology (Symmans et al., J Clin Oncol 2007;
25:4414-4422), incorporating primary tumor bed dimensions, cellularity, proportion
of in situ disease, number of positive nodes, and maximum nodal metastasis diameter.
RCB-II in TNBC is associated with intermediate risk of recurrence; this classification
has established prognostic and therapeutic implications (i.e., eligibility for adjuvant
cape per CREATE-X protocol and potential adjuvant pembro continuation
per KEYNOTE-522).

Miller-Payne grade 3 is assigned (moderate response; >30% but <90% reduction in
tumor cellularity). The marked decrease in TILs from 40% (pre-treatment) to 15%
(residual tumor) may reflect partial immunologic depletion at the tumor microenvironment
in the context of Keytruda treatment.

PD-L1 (22C3) result on the surgical specimen is pending; an addendum will be issued.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Maria Elena Reyes is a 45-year-old premenopausal Hispanic woman with Stage IIB (cT2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer of the left breast who presents approximately 6 weeks after left partial mastectomy and axillary lymph node dissection for post-operative wound check, final pathology review, and detailed discussion of adjuvant therapy planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Reyes was initially diagnosed with Stage IIB (cT2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer of the left breast after presenting with a palpable mass in the left upper outer quadrant. Core needle biopsy confirmed grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma, ER/PR/HER2-negative, Ki-67 85%, with a high TIL score of approximately 40%. PD-L1 testing by 22C3 antibody demonstrated a Combined Positive Score (CPS) of 18, establishing eligibility for pembrolizumab-based therapy. Axillary FNA was positive for metastatic carcinoma.

She was enrolled in treatment consistent with the KEYNOTE-522 trial schema and received neoadjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks combined with dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 q2 weeks for 4 cycles (AC phase), followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 for 12 doses with pembrolizumab continuing q3 weeks. A total of 6 pembrolizumab doses were administered during the neoadjuvant period.

The neoadjuvant course was notable for Grade 2 nausea during the AC phase (optimized with olanzapine-based antiemetic regimen), Grade 2 alopecia, and Grade 2 neutropenia without febrile episodes. During the paclitaxel phase, she developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy in bilateral hands and feet, prompting a 20% paclitaxel dose reduction for weeks 9–12 (from 80 to 64 mg/m2). She also developed Grade 2 immune-related arthralgia affecting bilateral knees and wrists, attributed to pembrolizumab, requiring a one-cycle hold of pembrolizumab, rheumatology consultation, and a 4-week course of prednisone 20 mg with taper. Pembrolizumab was ultimately resumed at full dose after improvement to Grade 1 arthralgia. Total neoadjuvant therapy duration was approximately 20 weeks.

Post-neoadjuvant MRI showed a partial radiographic response: the primary left breast mass decreased from 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm to 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm (approximately 60% size reduction by longest dimension), with improvement in axillary nodal morphology but persistent borderline abnormality in one node.

She underwent left partial mastectomy (wire-localized lumpectomy) with sentinel lymph node biopsy and completion axillary lymph node dissection approximately 6 weeks ago. Intraoperative frozen section of a sentinel node identified metastatic carcinoma, prompting ALND. A total of 16 lymph nodes were removed (3 sentinel, 13 additional axillary). There were no intraoperative or immediate post-operative complications.

Final surgical pathology demonstrated residual invasive ductal carcinoma, 1.2 cm, grade 3, at the excision site. Margins were all negative (closest 4 mm posteriorly). Three of 16 lymph nodes were positive for metastatic carcinoma (3/16, largest deposit 0.8 cm), with no extranodal extension. Lymphovascular invasion remained present in the residual tumor. TILs decreased to 15% from 40% pre-treatment, consistent with partial immunologic response. Miller-Payne grade 3, representing approximately 50–75% reduction in tumor cellularity. Residual Cancer Burden score was 2.8, classifying as RCB class II (intermediate residual disease). Final pathologic stage: ypT1cN1M0, Stage IIA. This constitutes a non-pathologic complete response (non-pCR) to neoadjuvant therapy. Residual tumor confirmed ER/PR/HER2-negative (HER2 IHC 1+), consistent with persistent triple-negative phenotype.

She presents today for wound assessment, full pathology review and discussion, and planning of adjuvant therapy.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

45-year-old woman with Stage IIB (cT2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer, left breast, now 6 weeks status post left partial mastectomy (breast-conserving surgery) and axillary lymph node dissection, with final pathology demonstrating non-pCR: ypT1cN1M0, Stage IIA, RCB class II (RCB score 2.8), 3 of 16 axillary nodes positive, residual 1.2 cm invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative phenotype confirmed on residual disease. She received 6 doses of pembrolizumab (Keytruda) during neoadjuvant therapy. The overall course was complicated by Grade 2 nausea, Grade 2 alopecia, Grade 2 CIPN (dose-reduced paclitaxel), Grade 2 immune-related arthralgia (held pembrolizumab x1 cycle, treated with steroids), and Grade 2 neutropenia without fever.

At today's visit, wound is healing well, and we have a thorough discussion regarding pathologic findings and implications. I take significant time to explain to the patient that while she had a very good partial response to chemotherapy and immunotherapy, the residual cancer that remained at surgery (though small) is meaningful from a prognostic standpoint, and that standard-of-care adjuvant therapy is strongly indicated. I reassure her that there are proven effective adjuvant options that materially reduce her risk of recurrence.

1. PATHOLOGY DISCUSSION — NON-pCR, RESIDUAL DISEASE:
Final surgical pathology reviewed in detail with the patient. Residual 1.2 cm IDC, 3/16 positive nodes, RCB-II, ypT1cN1M0. This represents a meaningful but incomplete response to neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/chemotherapy. The residual cancer retains triple-negative biomarker status, which is both expected and relevant to adjuvant planning. We discuss the CREATE-X and KEYNOTE-522 trial data showing benefit of escalated adjuvant therapy in patients with residual disease after neoadjuvant treatment.

2. ADJUVANT CAPECITABINE (Xeloda) — CREATE-X:
Based on the CREATE-X trial data demonstrating significant improvement in disease-free and overall survival with adjuvant capecitabine in patients with HER2-negative breast cancer and residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy (with the greatest benefit seen in TNBC), we will initiate adjuvant capecitabine:
— Dose: 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily (based on BSA 1.73 m2 = approximately 1700 mg per dose, rounded to 1500 mg BID per institutional dosing table for convenience and tolerability).
— Schedule: Days 1–14 of a 21-day cycle.
— Duration: 8 cycles total (approximately 6 months).
— DPYD testing confirms no contraindication; safe to proceed.
— Preemptive counseling provided regarding hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia), diarrhea, mucositis, and fatigue. Instructed to begin urea cream prophylactically and to avoid heat exposure to hands and feet. Will monitor closely.
— Drug interactions reviewed: no current interacting medications.
— Capecitabine will be coordinated with radiation — per institutional protocol, capecitabine will be held during the concurrent radiation period to minimize radiation dermatitis and mucositis, then resumed after completion of RT.

3. ADJUVANT PEMBROLIZUMAB CONTINUATION — KEYNOTE-522:
Per the KEYNOTE-522 protocol, patients receive a total of approximately 1 year of pembrolizumab (17 total doses at q3-week intervals). The patient has received 6 doses during the neoadjuvant phase. We will continue pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks for approximately 9 additional doses in the adjuvant setting, to complete the full 17-dose course (approximately 1 year total from initiation).
— No dose modification needed at this time; prior grade 2 immune-related arthralgia resolved and pembrolizumab had been successfully restarted.
— Immune-related adverse event monitoring will continue at each cycle: comprehensive metabolic panel, TSH, CBC, and symptom review.
— Patient educated on signs of immune-related adverse events to watch for (colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, dermatitis, endocrinopathies).
— First adjuvant pembrolizumab dose to be scheduled within the next week to 10 days.

4. RADIATION ONCOLOGY — ADJUVANT RADIATION:
Referral placed to radiation oncology today for adjuvant radiation therapy. Given 3 positive lymph nodes (3/16 positive), breast-conserving surgery, and residual nodal disease, adjuvant regional nodal irradiation is strongly indicated per NCCN and ASCO/ASTRO guidelines in addition to whole-breast irradiation with tumor bed boost.
— Expected fields: whole left breast with tumor bed boost, left supraclavicular fossa, axillary levels I–III, and consideration of internal mammary chain irradiation.
— Radiation will be sequenced to begin after adequate wound healing (typically 6–8 weeks post-surgery; patient is now at 6 weeks with satisfactory wound healing; radiation oncology will make final determination on timing).
— Capecitabine will be held during the radiation course per protocol.

5. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY — GRADE 1 CIPN:
Residual Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy from paclitaxel, bilateral hands and feet. Currently not affecting function. No intervention beyond watchful monitoring at this time. Expectation of gradual improvement over months. Will reassess at each visit. Patient instructed in fall precautions.

6. LEFT ARM LYMPHEDEMA RISK:
Borderline asymmetry in left upper extremity circumference (1.5 cm greater than contralateral). Will refer to lymphedema therapy for baseline assessment and prophylactic education. Compression sleeve during prolonged air travel recommended.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL / QUALITY OF LIFE:
Patient is understandably distressed by the non-pCR finding and concerns about recurrence. Actively engaged in therapy and peer support group, which I strongly encourage. We spend additional time discussing the rationale for ongoing adjuvant therapy, the cumulative benefit, and the evidence base. I emphasize that despite the non-pCR, she is young, fit, and has received aggressive multimodal treatment. Social work referral offered; patient declined at this time but will consider.

8. RETURN VISIT:
Follow-up in 3 weeks for first adjuvant pembrolizumab infusion and capecitabine initiation visit (laboratory review, toxicity check). Radiation oncology consultation to be completed within the next 1–2 weeks.

Cancer Status: Post-surgery, residual disease confirmed (non-pCR, RCB-II). Adjuvant therapy initiated (pembrolizumab + capecitabine), radiation oncology referral placed. Active treatment phase beginning.

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes total, including detailed counseling regarding pathology, adjuvant therapy rationale, expectations, and side effect management.

DIAGNOSTIC MAMMOGRAPHY AND TARGETED BREAST ULTRASOUND WITH LEFT AXILLARY ULTRASOUND

CLINICAL INDICATION:
A 45-year-old premenopausal woman presenting with a self-detected palpable mass in the left breast, upper outer quadrant, of uncertain duration. Referred by primary care for diagnostic workup. No prior mammographic history on file at this institution. No personal history of breast cancer. Family history: maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 52. Clinical exam reportedly notable for a firm, irregular left breast mass and a palpable left axillary lymph node.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
MODALITY: Digital full-field diagnostic mammography, bilateral (standard CC and MLO projections with additional spot compression and magnification views of the left breast); followed by targeted hand-held B-mode ultrasound of the left breast and bilateral axillae.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

MAMMOGRAPHIC FINDINGS:

Left Breast:
The breast parenchyma is heterogeneously dense (ACR density category C), which may lower sensitivity for detection of small masses.

There is a dominant, irregular mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast measuring approximately 3.4 cm in greatest dimension on the CC projection. The mass demonstrates spiculated margins with associated architectural distortion of the surrounding parenchyma, radiating trabecular distortion extending for several centimeters from the epicenter of the mass. The mass is located at approximately the 10 o'clock position, corresponding to the palpable clinical abnormality, marked with a radiopaque BB marker at the time of imaging. The lesion appears isodense to mildly hyperdense relative to surrounding breast tissue. Spot compression views confirm the mass is real and not a summation artifact; the spiculated margins persist and are accentuated on compression.

No suspicious microcalcifications are identified within or adjacent to the mass. No coarse or dystrophic calcifications. No pleomorphic or fine linear branching calcification clusters elsewhere in the breast.

No additional suspicious masses or focal asymmetries are identified in the left breast on any projection. No nipple retraction or skin thickening appreciated on mammographic views, though clinical evaluation is necessary to confirm.

Right Breast:
No suspicious masses, architectural distortion, or microcalcifications. No focal asymmetry. No dominant density. No suspicious axillary adenopathy appreciated on the right MLO projection.

Left Axilla (MLO projection):
There is at least one morphologically suspicious-appearing lymph node visible in the left axilla on the MLO view, with apparent cortical thickening. Further characterization provided on ultrasound (see below).

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
ULTRASOUND FINDINGS:

Left Breast — Targeted Evaluation:
A hypoechoic irregular mass is identified at the 10 o'clock position of the left breast, approximately 3-4 cm from the nipple, corresponding precisely to the palpable clinical abnormality and the mammographic mass. The lesion measures 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm (transverse x anteroposterior x craniocaudal). The margins are angulated and microlobulated with an indistinct interface between the mass and the surrounding stroma in places. Marked posterior acoustic shadowing is present, consistent with a densely fibrotic or infiltrating lesion. The mass is taller-than-wide in the anti-radial plane, a feature further raising concern for malignancy. Increased vascularity is demonstrated within and at the periphery of the mass on color Doppler interrogation. No cystic or necrotic components are identified.

No additional satellite lesions or separate suspicious nodules are identified in the left breast on the targeted survey of the upper outer quadrant and surrounding breast tissue. No intramammary lymph nodes with abnormal morphology.

Right Breast:
Limited survey of the right breast performed. No discrete suspicious masses or architectural distortion identified. Multiple small simple cysts noted bilaterally, not clinically significant.

Left Axilla — Ultrasound Evaluation:
Multiple axillary lymph nodes are visualized in the left axilla. One node is clearly morphologically abnormal: it measures approximately 2.0 x 1.0 cm and demonstrates diffuse cortical thickening to approximately 8 mm (normal cortex < 3 mm), with complete loss of the central fatty hilum, giving the node a rounded, hypoechoic appearance. No hilar vascularity is identified; vascularity is peripheral and cortical on Doppler assessment. A second node at level I shows mild cortical asymmetry (cortex 4 mm) and is indeterminate but suspicious. No level II or III adenopathy is appreciated on this limited survey.

Right Axilla:
Axillary lymph nodes visualized on the right are within normal size and morphologic limits. Fatty hila preserved bilaterally. No cortical thickening.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION:

1. LEFT BREAST MASS, 10 O'CLOCK POSITION, 4 CM FROM NIPPLE: Large irregular spiculated mass on mammography measuring approximately 3.4 cm, confirmed by ultrasound as a 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm hypoechoic angulated mass with posterior acoustic shadowing. The constellation of features — spiculation, architectural distortion, posterior shadowing, increased internal vascularity, and taller-than-wide orientation — is highly suspicious for invasive malignancy. No microcalcifications to suggest an extensive intraductal component.

2. LEFT AXILLARY ADENOPATHY: At least one morphologically abnormal left level I lymph node with markedly thickened cortex (approximately 8 mm) and complete loss of fatty hilum, appearance highly concerning for nodal metastatic involvement. A second level I node is indeterminate.

3. RIGHT BREAST AND RIGHT AXILLA: No suspicious findings.

BI-RADS CATEGORY: 5 — Highly suggestive of malignancy. Appropriate action: tissue sampling.

RECOMMENDATION: Ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy of the left breast mass and ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration or core biopsy of the morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph node are recommended for histopathologic diagnosis and nodal staging. Correlation with clinical exam and multidisciplinary evaluation recommended.

Patient: Maria Elena Reyes

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Routine oncology follow-up during adjuvant systemic therapy. Patient presents for Cycle 5 of adjuvant capecitabine and interval assessment. She has also been noted to have a mildly elevated TSH on recent labs, prompting discussion today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Reyes is a 45-year-old premenopausal woman with a diagnosis of Stage IIB TNBC of the left breast established approximately 14 months ago. She is well known to our service. To briefly summarize her treatment course: she completed neoadjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks (6 doses during neoadjuvant phase) in combination with dose-dense AC (doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide, 4 cycles q2 weeks), followed by weekly paclitaxel (12 doses; reduced to 64 mg/m2 x 4 doses for Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy) over approximately 20 total weeks of neoadjuvant therapy. She then underwent left breast-conserving surgery (partial mastectomy with wire localization) and completion axillary lymph node dissection. Surgical pathology confirmed residual disease: ypT1cN1, with a 1.2 cm focus of residual invasive ductal carcinoma and 3 of 16 positive lymph nodes (RCB score 2.8, RCB-II, non-pCR). She subsequently completed adjuvant locoregional radiation to the left breast, supraclavicular fossa, axilla, and internal mammary chain (whole breast 50 Gy + 10 Gy boost; regional nodal irradiation included), finishing approximately 6 weeks post-surgery. She developed Grade 2 radiation dermatitis during RT (managed with silver sulfadiazine and a brief treatment break) and Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis approximately 6 weeks after RT completion (managed with low-dose prednisone with taper, now resolved).

Following radiation, she transitioned to adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 PO BID x 14 days/21-day cycle (per CREATE-X), along with continuation of pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks for approximately 1 year total (neoadjuvant + adjuvant). DPYD genotyping was performed prior to capecitabine initiation and was wildtype, confirming no dose adjustment needed on that basis. During Cycle 3 of capecitabine, she developed Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) with bilateral palmar/plantar erythema, swelling, and tenderness, as well as Grade 2 diarrhea (4–6 loose stools per day above baseline). Capecitabine was dose-reduced by 25% to 750 mg/m2 BID for Cycle 3 and onward. Diarrhea was managed with loperamide and resolved within 5 days. Hand-foot syndrome subsequently improved.

She is now presenting for Cycle 5, Day 1 of capecitabine at the reduced dose of 750 mg/m2 BID. She has received 14 of 17 planned total pembrolizumab doses and is on track to complete her full year of pembro. She is today scheduled to receive pembrolizumab dose 14 as well, as cycles align today.

Of note, routine labs drawn last week revealed a TSH of 7.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated) with a free T4 in the low-normal range. This is a new finding compared to prior TSH values, which have been within normal limits throughout therapy. Given her prolonged pembrolizumab exposure, this is most consistent with immune-mediated hypothyroidism, a recognized and expected immune-related adverse event (irAE) of checkpoint inhibitor therapy. She denies frank hypothyroid symptoms — no significant fatigue beyond her treatment baseline, no cold intolerance, no constipation, no weight gain, no myalgias or proximal muscle weakness, no cognitive changes, and no edema. However, she does report feeling somewhat more tired than her prior baseline over the past few weeks, which she had attributed to ongoing capecitabine. It is possible the subclinical hypothyroidism is contributing.

Surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed approximately one month ago showed no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Post-radiation changes in the left lung base (minor resolving ground-glass opacity, consistent with prior radiation pneumonitis) were noted and deemed stable/improving. No hepatic lesions, no new adenopathy, no osseous abnormalities.

She reports that hand-foot syndrome has settled to what she describes as Grade 1 — mild redness and tenderness of her palms and soles that is present but manageable and does not interfere with her ability to work or perform daily activities. She has been compliant with urea-based emollient cream (Eucerin) twice daily and is avoiding heat/friction per instructions. She denies any new gastrointestinal complaints (no diarrhea, no mucositis, no nausea). Peripheral neuropathy from paclitaxel persists as a very mild tingling in the fingertips, bothering her only occasionally; she grades it as Grade 1 and stable.

She has returned to part-time teaching. Her hair has begun to regrow. She is emotionally engaged with her support group and continues individual therapy.

Patient has received 14 of a planned 17 total pembrolizumab doses (6 during neoadjuvant therapy + 8 adjuvant doses to date), consistent with KEYNOTE-522 protocol targeting approximately 1 year of total pembrolizumab. Immune review performed: no colitis (no diarrhea), no active pneumonitis (lungs clear, prior radiation pneumonitis resolved), no hepatitis (LFTs normal), no adrenal insufficiency (morning cortisol normal), no new arthralgias (prior irAE resolved), no skin toxicity beyond expected. New elevation in TSH (7.8 mIU/L) with low-normal free T4 in the setting of ongoing pembrolizumab. Per KEYNOTE-522 protocol and standard irAE management guidelines, hypothyroidism of this degree (TSH elevated but below 10, patient asymptomatic) does not require pembrolizumab interruption. Ms. Reyes is a 46-year-old premenopausal woman of Hispanic/Latina ethnicity, originally from Mexico, residing in the southwestern United States, who works as a high school teacher. Given non-pCR per CREATE-X trial rationale, she was started on adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 every 21 days (dose reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID from Cycle 3 onward for Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome/palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) and continued pembrolizumab to complete 1 year of total pembro therapy (~17 doses total across neoadjuvant and adjuvant phases). She also received adjuvant radiation to the left breast and regional lymphatics (whole breast 50 Gy + 10 Gy tumor bed boost; regional nodal irradiation to supraclavicular, axillary levels I-III, and internal mammary chain), complicated by Grade 2 radiation dermatitis with moist desquamation and a mild Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis managed conservatively with a short prednisone course.

During the adjuvant phase, she developed pembrolizumab-related immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH peak 7.8 mIU/L), managed with levothyroxine, ultimately dose-stabilized at 75 mcg daily with TSH normalization. Pembrolizumab was continued without interruption for the hypothyroidism per protocol guidance. She completed all 8 cycles of capecitabine and all 17 planned pembrolizumab doses approximately 12 months ago.

Surveillance to date: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at 6 months post-surgery showed no evidence of disease (NED), with resolving left lung post-radiation changes. CT CAP and bilateral breast MRI at 12 months post-surgery were both without evidence of local recurrence or distant metastasis. Breast MRI performed at 18 months (obtained and reviewed in preparation for today's visit) again showed no suspicious new enhancement, stable post-operative and post-radiation changes in the left breast, and no pathologic axillary adenopathy bilaterally.

Today at 18 months post-surgery, Ms. Reyes reports feeling well. She has returned to full-time teaching. She no longer has peripheral neuropathy symptoms, which resolved completely over several months after completion of paclitaxel. Hand-foot syndrome has fully resolved. Her hair has fully regrown. She is well-compensated on levothyroxine. She mentions mild fatigue intermittently but describes it as manageable and improved compared to prior visits. She has been actively engaged in a support group for young women with breast cancer and continues individual psychotherapy. She reports normal menstrual cycles have resumed (chemotherapy-associated amenorrhea resolved at approximately 9 months post-completion of chemotherapy). She was referred to reproductive endocrinology previously for counseling regarding ovarian function and pregnancy safety post-TNBC; she underwent that evaluation and is in ongoing discussion with her RE.

She had an expanded germline panel sent at the 12-month visit. Results confirmed BRCA1 and BRCA2 wildtype, PALB2 wildtype, RAD51C and RAD51D wildtype, BRIP1 wildtype, ATM p.V2424G (variant of uncertain significance, classification unchanged since original testing). Genetic counselor reviewed these results with the patient at a recent appointment; no change in surgical or screening management was recommended on the basis of the ATM VUS.

She also had a baseline echocardiogram at the 12-month visit (LVEF 62%, normal), obtained for cardiovascular surveillance given her prior anthracycline exposure.

Today's visit, bilateral breast MRI, and recent labs are all without evidence of local or distant recurrence. She is clinically well with ECOG PS 0. This is an encouraging milestone. We discussed that the highest risk period for TNBC recurrence is within the first 3 years from completion of therapy, and that ongoing close surveillance is important during this window.

- Continue surveillance per schedule: clinic visits every 3-4 months through the 2-year mark (approximately 6 months from now), then transition to every 6 months through year 5.

Specimen A: Received in formalin labeled with patient name, medical record number, and "left breast mass, 10 o'clock." --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Specimen A - Left Breast Core Needle Biopsy:

Sections of the core biopsy material show fragments of fibroadipose and fibrous breast stroma with foci of benign ductal epithelium. The majority of the cores are extensively replaced by a high-grade invasive carcinoma. The neoplastic cells are arranged predominantly in solid nests, sheets, and single infiltrating cords with only rare and abortive tubular structures identified (tubule score 3/3). The neoplastic cells are large and polygonal with moderate to abundant amounts of pale eosinophilic cytoplasm. Nuclei are markedly enlarged, vesicular, and pleomorphic with irregular contours, coarsely clumped chromatin, and conspicuous often cherry-red macronucleoli; this corresponds to a high nuclear grade (nuclear score 3/3). Mitotic figures are readily identified and numerous, counting greater than 20 per 10 high-power fields in the most active areas, including atypical mitoses (mitotic score 3/3). The combined Nottingham histologic grade is 9/9 (Grade 3, poorly differentiated). The overall histologic pattern, cytomorphology, and immunophenotype (see below) are diagnostic of invasive carcinoma of no special type (invasive ductal carcinoma, NOS), grade 3.

Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocytes (TILs): A brisk stromal lymphocytic infiltrate is present, predominantly composed of lymphocytes and lesser plasma cells distributed within the tumor stroma between tumor cell nests. TIL density is estimated at approximately 40% (moderate-to-high, per International TIL Working Group criteria). No intraepithelial TILs are noted.

Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS): No ductal carcinoma in situ is identified within the submitted core biopsy tissue.

Lymphovascular Invasion (LVI): Unequivocal lymphovascular invasion is present, identified as cohesive clusters of carcinoma cells within endothelium-lined vascular spaces at the periphery of the invasive tumor, confirmed on immunohistochemical staining with D2-40 (podoplanin) and CD31, which highlights lymphatic and vascular channels containing tumor emboli.

Necrosis: Focal central comedonecrosis-type necrosis is present within some of the larger solid tumor nests.

Background Breast Parenchyma: The non-neoplastic breast parenchyma shows fibrocystic changes with mild columnar cell change. No atypical ductal hyperplasia is present in non-neoplastic areas.

Specimen B - Left Axillary Lymph Node FNA Cytology:

Smears and cell block sections show highly cellular material. Individually dispersed and loosely cohesive clusters of large atypical epithelioid cells are identified with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, irregular nuclear membranes, prominent nucleoli, and brisk mitotic activity. The cytomorphologic features are consistent with metastatic carcinoma, morphologically similar to the left breast primary. Lymphoid background is present but markedly reduced, consistent with replacement of nodal architecture by metastatic carcinoma. No discrete lymphoid follicular fragments are identified.

Conclusion, Specimen B: Positive for malignant cells, consistent with metastatic carcinoma (see note).

Study requested for pre-treatment baseline assessment, extent-of-disease evaluation, and surgical planning prior to initiation of neoadjuvant systemic therapy.

COMPARISON: Diagnostic mammogram and breast ultrasound (same encounter, obtained approximately 10 days prior). No prior breast MRI available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Dedicated bilateral breast MRI performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet using a dedicated 16-channel bilateral breast coil. Patient positioned prone. Protocol included: axial pre-contrast T1-weighted and T2-weighted fat-suppressed sequences; axial dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) T1-weighted fat-suppressed sequences acquired pre-contrast and at 60-second intervals through five post-contrast phases following intravenous administration of gadobutrol (Gadavist) 0.1 mmol/kg. Maximum intensity projection (MIP) reconstructions, subtraction images, and kinetic color mapping were generated. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI, b = 0 and 800 s/mm2) with corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping also performed. Sagittal and coronal reformats obtained for lesion characterization.

---

FINDINGS:

LEFT BREAST:

Primary Mass: There is an irregular, heterogeneously enhancing mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast, centered at the 10 o'clock position, approximately 4 cm from the nipple. The mass measures 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm in the axial plane and craniocaudal dimension (previously measured at 3.2 cm on clinical exam and 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm on sonography). Morphology: the margins are irregular and non-circumscribed, with spiculated extensions into the surrounding parenchyma on both MIP and subtraction images. The internal architecture demonstrates heterogeneous non-uniform enhancement with multiple internal hypointense septations on T1 post-contrast sequences. T2-weighted signal within the mass is mildly elevated relative to surrounding glandular tissue, without a well-defined rim or cystic component. Enhancement kinetics: rapid initial enhancement on the first post-contrast acquisition (type III kinetic curve), with a washout pattern on delayed phases, a feature highly associated with malignant breast lesions. On kinetic color-overlay mapping, the dominant enhancing component demonstrates predominant washout (red voxels) throughout the central portion of the mass, with a small peripheral rim of plateau-type kinetics (yellow voxels). On DWI, the lesion demonstrates restricted diffusion with markedly low ADC values (mean ADC approximately 0.82 x 10^-3 mm2/s), consistent with high cellularity. No internal fat signal to suggest fat necrosis or fibro-adenoma.

Relationship to Adjacent Structures: No thickening or direct involvement of the overlying skin. The skin overlying the mass appears intact and is not retracted or nodular. Nipple: the nipple-areolar complex is uninvolved; no retroareolar extension or direct nipple-ductal spread identified. The deep margin of the mass approaches the anterior pectoralis fascia but does not abut or involve it; there is a small plane of intervening glandular tissue. No chest wall invasion. No pectoralis major involvement.

Additional Left Breast Findings: No additional suspicious enhancing foci, satellite nodules, or non-mass enhancement identified elsewhere in the left breast parenchyma. No evidence of multifocal or multicentric disease. There is no intracanalicular ductal spread or pathologically enhancing ductal system. No suspicious segmental non-mass enhancement. Background parenchymal enhancement: mildly scattered (BPE grade: mild), appropriate for a premenopausal woman.

Left Axilla: Multiple lymph nodes are identified in the left axilla at levels I and II. Three lymph nodes demonstrate morphologic abnormality:
— The largest abnormal node is located at level I and measures approximately 1.9 cm in short-axis diameter. It demonstrates cortical thickening (estimated cortical thickness 7–9 mm) with near-complete effacement of the fatty hilum, and shows avid enhancement on post-contrast sequences. The overall shape is rounded rather than reniform.
— A second level I node measures 1.3 cm in short-axis diameter with focal cortical thickening to approximately 6 mm and partial hilar effacement. Enhancement is moderate.
— A third level I node, more medially positioned, is borderline in size at 1.0 cm with mild diffuse cortical thickening (4 mm) without complete hilar loss. Enhancement is mild to moderate; this node is regarded as suspicious rather than definitively abnormal but is included given clinical context.
These findings are consistent with metastatic nodal involvement, concordant with prior FNA cytology confirming malignancy. No level II or level III axillary adenopathy. No infraclavicular adenopathy identified.

Internal Mammary Region (Left): No abnormally enlarged or enhancing lymph nodes identified along the left internal mammary chain.

RIGHT BREAST:

No suspicious mass, irregular enhancement, or non-mass enhancement identified. Background parenchymal enhancement is mild and symmetric. Fibroglandular tissue distribution: scattered fibroglandular densities. No architectural distortion. No suspicious lymph nodes in the right axilla; nodes present are normal in morphology, with preserved fatty hila and oval reniform shape, the largest measuring 0.8 cm in short axis. No right internal mammary adenopathy.

ADDITIONAL OBSERVATIONS:

No skin thickening or skin edema in either breast. No chest wall or pectoral muscle signal abnormality bilaterally. Visible portions of the sternum, ribs, and thoracic spine included in the field of view demonstrate no suspicious osseous signal abnormality. Minimal visualized lung bases without discrete parenchymal lesions.

Left breast primary malignancy: Irregular, heterogeneously enhancing mass in the left upper outer quadrant at 10 o'clock, measuring 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm, with washout enhancement kinetics, restricted diffusion, and spiculated margins — morphologically aggressive, consistent with known biopsy-proven invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative subtype. 4. MRI staging: cT2 N1 M0 — locally advanced left breast cancer confined to the breast and left axilla, consistent with clinical staging. No occult contralateral malignancy. No internal mammary or distant nodal involvement on this examination.

INTEGRATED GENOMIC INTERPRETATION
——————————————————————————————————————————

This tumor harbors a genomic profile highly characteristic of aggressive, treatment-refractory triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) of the basal-like molecular subtype. The co-occurrence of the following features defines a high-risk genomic landscape:

  1. TP53 p.R248W (hotspot, clonal): Confirms p53 pathway inactivation as a truncal event, consistent with essentially universal TP53 alteration in TNBC. The dominant-negative and gain-of-function properties of this specific substitution may additionally promote oncogenic transcriptional programs.

  2. PTEN homozygous deletion + PI3K pathway activation: In the absence of PIK3CA and AKT1 mutations, PTEN loss is the dominant PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activating mechanism. This may serve as a rationale for clinical trial enrollment targeting AKT (e.g., AKT inhibitors ipatasertib or capivasertib in combination with chemotherapy or immunotherapy).

  3. Cell cycle dysregulation (RB1 biallelic inactivation + CDKN2A homozygous deletion + CDK6 amplification + MYC amplification): The compound disruption of the G1/S checkpoint through independent mechanisms (loss of both major checkpoint suppressors RB1 and p16INK4a, combined with amplification of their activators CDK6 and MYC) indicates extreme proliferative capacity and near-complete abrogation of cell cycle checkpoints. Ki-67 85% is the phenotypic correlate of this genomic state.

  4. Chromatin remodeling alterations (KMT2C, ARID1A): Concurrent loss-of-function in two SWI/SNF and COMPASS-related chromatin regulators suggests epigenetic deregulation as a contributor to malignant transcriptional programs. These alterations may potentiate inflammatory/immunogenic gene expression and could partially account for the elevated TIL score (40%) and PD-L1 positivity (CPS 18) observed on pathology.

  5. Subclonal heterogeneity (SPEN, FAT1, NF1, NOTCH1 at VAF 15-22%): The presence of multiple subclonal alterations involving the Notch, RAS, and Wnt pathways indicates tumor evolutionary complexity. This heterogeneity may facilitate adaptive resistance under therapeutic selective pressure and is consistent with the high genomic instability observed on copy number analysis.

  6. Borderline HRD (score 42, just below threshold): While formal HRD-high designation is not met, the elevated chromosomal structural instability (28% genome altered, near-triploid ploidy, multiple focal deletions) is consistent with a tumor with at least partial HR pathway impairment. Platinum-based chemotherapy sensitivity may be greater than average for TNBC, even in the absence of BRCA mutation.

  7. PD-L1 CPS 18 (positive): Supports immunotherapy eligibility and is biologically consistent with the elevated TIL score and chromatin remodeling defects. The immune-inflamed tumor microenvironment may potentiate response to pembrolizumab-based regimens.

SUMMARY STATEMENT:
Comprehensive genomic profiling reveals a highly complex, clonally evolved TNBC genome with truncal TP53 mutation (p.R248W), PTEN homozygous deletion, high-level MYC amplification, biallelic RB1 inactivation, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion as the dominant oncogenic drivers. PD-L1 CPS 18 supports immunotherapy eligibility. TMB is intermediate (8 mut/Mb), MSS confirmed. HRD score is borderline (42, below 42.5 threshold). No germline BRCA1/2/PALB2 pathogenic variants identified; ATM p.V2424G remains a VUS. No actionable fusions or rearrangements identified. The overall genomic profile is consistent with high-grade, highly aggressive TNBC and provides biological rationale for pembrolizumab-containing neoadjuvant regimens (e.g., KEYNOTE-522). Clinical trial consideration for AKT/PI3K pathway inhibitors is supported given PTEN loss.
